title: practical english usage - Part 247
content:
In a formal style, requests, invitations, suggestions, etc are often introduced by 3  We    would       like    to   ...

I'd quite like to start thinking about going home.

I'm thinking of going to London tomorrow.

I think I'll go to bed in a few minutes.

intentions directly.

We   can say that we  are thinking of doing things, instead of expressing our 2 I think      I'll ...

For more examples, ►    301.3.

This music's kind of boring.     Will you be seeing Denise at all?

The food's a bit expensive.      They don't seem very friendly.

I find her rather bossy, don't you?  Maybe I'll go for a walk now.

He's quite difficult to understand, isn't he?

seem, at all (►   387).

politely) by using softening expressions like quite, rather, kind of, a bit, maybe, We   can express our opinions and intentions less directly (and therefore more 1  quite,      maybe,        I think,      etc 312 politeness:       softening       expressions grammar •  313    pronunciation: stress and rhythm particular f/pə'tɪkjələ(r)/) - PARticle f/'pu:tikl/J conFUSED f/kɔn'fju:zd/j - CONfident (/'konfidɔnt/j exPERience (/ɪk'spiərɪəns/j - EXpert f/'ekspɜ:t/j preFER (/pn'fɜɪ(r)/) - PREference f/'prefrəns/,) Compare the first syllables in the following pairs of words: prefixes written with e, like de-, re-, pre-, ex-) or /ə/   (in other cases).

Unstressed syllables nearly always have one of two vowels: /ɪ/  (in unstressed 4 stress     and   pronunciation a DARK blue SUIT - It's dark BLUE.

She SAT down and cried.

- Do sit DOWN.

Money problems BROKE up their marriage.

- Their marriage broke UP.

Many short phrases - for instance, two-word verbs - have variable stress.

The year NiNEteen TWENty - nineTEEN jApanese cooking - JapanESE It's time for my AFternoon NAP. (stress at the beginning) - afterNOON (stress at the end) is in a sentence, especially if another stressed word follows. Compare: the word is spoken alone, but it can move to an earlier position when the word Some words have variable stress. In these, the stress is at or near the end when 3 variable      stress A  good dictionary will show how  words and common phrases are stressed.

PHOtograph phoTOgrapher photoGRAphic to inCREASE an iNcrease Related words can have different stress patterns.

front DOOR  (not FRONT door) Living room (not living ROOM) Many short phrases also have a fixed stress pattern.

(►  314  below).

The  stressed syllable of a word is the one that can carry an intonation movement enterTAIN, underSTAND, concenTRAtion Stressed on third syllable: inSTEAD, proNOUNCE, aGREEment, particularly Stressed on second syllable: AFter, CApital, HAPpen, Exercise, EAsy Stressed on first syllable: and pronunciation. Examples: usually has to learn the stress pattern of a word along with its meaning, spelling There are not many rules to show which  syllable of a word will be stressed: one English words with more than one syllable mostly have a fixed stress pattern.

conjunctions and auxiliary verbs.

adjectives and adverbs, and less stress to pronouns, determiners, prepositions, in speech. In sentences, we  usually give more stress to nouns, ordinary verbs, Rhythm is the word for the way stressed and unstressed syllables make patterns 6 stress     in  sentences;       rhythm Megan called me YESTERDAY. (Not today.) Megan called ME yesterday. (But she didn’ t call you, did she?) Megan CALLED me yesterday. (But she didn’ t come  and see me.) MEGAN  called me yesterday. (She was the one who  called.) Changes in stress can affect the meaning of a sentence. Compare: am I'm really tired. I really   tired.
